g.text: B-tree vs B+tree: Same 15 Keys, Different Structure
Data location | All nodes | Leaves only
Internal node payload | key + row data (100B) | key + child ptr (12B)
Keys per internal page | ~35 (with 100B rows) | ~340 (keys only)
Point lookup I/O | 1-3 pages (may hit internal) | Always 3 pages (must reach leaf)
Range scan WHERE k BETWEEN 20 AND 50 | Zigzag traversal (touch every level) | Descend once then follow leaf chain
Leaf connectivity | None (use parent stack) | Doubly-linked list
Write cost | One insert per node level | One insert per node level
Best for | Rowid point lookups (table storage) | Range scans and secondary indexes
B-tree for TABLES | Key = rowid (integer) | Fast: SELECT * FROM t WHERE rowid = 42
B-tree for TABLES | Row data lives IN the node | No double lookup needed for rowid access
B+tree for INDEXES | Key = indexed column value | Fast: WHERE age BETWEEN 20 AND 30 (leaf chain)
B+tree for INDEXES | Leaf stores (col_value, rowid) | Double lookup: index leaf -> rowid -> table B-tree
B+tree for INDEXES | Higher fanout = shallower tree | 340^3 = 39M keys in 3 levels vs 35^3 = 43K
Data stored in ALL nodes (internal + leaf)
Max fanout: 3 (data in nodes reduces fanout)
Point lookup: may stop at internal node
Range scan: zigzag traversal up and down tree
No leaf links -- range scan requires stack
Used by: SQLite table storage (rowid lookup)
Data stored ONLY in leaf nodes (internals are signposts)
Fanout: 5 (no data payload -- more keys fit)
Total pages: 15 (more pages, but shallower at scale)
Max fanout: 5 (no data in internals -- more keys fit)
Point lookup: always descends to leaf
Range scan: follow leaf chain sequentially
Leaf links enable fast BETWEEN / ORDER BY
Used by: SQLite secondary indexes, PostgreSQL, InnoDB
